**Woolley & Fishbach (2015), "The Experience Matters More Than You Think: People Value Intrinsic Incentives More Inside Than Outside an Activity" (JPSP)** — People systematically underweight how much intrinsic enjoyment will matter when choosing activities in advance, but it strongly predicts actual persistence and satisfaction once they're doing the work. **Heath (1999), "On the Social Psychology of Agency Relationships: Lay Theories of Motivation Overemphasize Extrinsic Incentives" (OBHDP)** — People believe others (and to some degree themselves) are driven mainly by pay and status, while actual motivation is dominated by intrinsic factors. **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)** — Across 86 studies, pay level correlates with job satisfaction at only about r = .15. A modestly higher salary is unlikely to move your day-to-day satisfaction much.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (American Psychologist)** — Well-being at work depends on autonomy, competence, and relatedness, not just interest in the task content. Notably, leading a team can satisfy these needs too — so the Meta offer isn't purely an "extrinsic" option.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory" (OBHDP)** — Job satisfaction is driven by skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback — a broader bundle than "interestingness." A leadership role can score higher on several of these dimensions even if the topic is duller.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" (Journal of Research in Personality)** — People who view work as a calling report higher life and job satisfaction than those oriented toward advancement or pay, largely independent of occupation. **Wilson & Gilbert (2003), "Affective Forecasting" (Advances in Experimental Social Psychology)** — People reliably mispredict the intensity and duration of future emotions, including overestimating how long both excitement and regret will last. **Brickman, Coates & Janoff-Bulman (1978), "Lottery Winners and Accident Victims: Is Happiness Relative?" (JPSP)** — Major positive changes in circumstances produce smaller and shorter-lived happiness gains than expected due to hedonic adaptation. A salary increase is among the things people adapt to fastest; engaging daily activities tend to be more adaptation-resistant.

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005), "Consequences of Individuals' Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis" (Personnel Psychology)** — Person–job and person–organization fit are among the strongest predictors of satisfaction, commitment, and retention. The real question may be less "interesting vs. leadership" and which environment fits *you* better overall.

Net read: your friends are directionally right (interest is underrated, pay is overrated), but "almost exclusively" overstates it — autonomy, growth, and the long-desired leadership opportunity are exactly the kinds of intrinsic factors the same literature says matter.

answer:
Short answer woven in via the papers: the research supports your friends *directionally* (intrinsic interest matters more than people expect, money less), but not "almost exclusively."

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) — "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature"**
Across 92 samples, pay level correlated with job satisfaction at only r ≈ .15, meaning salary differences explain very little of how happy people are at work. This supports the idea that a modest pay bump is a weak reason to switch jobs.

**Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012) — "Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research"**
This meta-analysis found that the fit between a person's interests and their work meaningfully predicts both job performance and persistence. So "interestingness" isn't just hedonics — it predicts how well you'll actually do.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014) — "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"**
Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, but extrinsic incentives still matter, especially for quantity-type outcomes. This is evidence against the "almost exclusively" framing — both factors operate jointly.

**Ryan & Deci (2000) — "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"**
Well-being and sustained motivation depend on satisfying needs for autonomy, competence, and *relatedness* — not interest alone. Notably, leading a team (something you say you've always wanted) can serve autonomy and competence needs too, so the Meta offer isn't purely an "extrinsic" option.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997) — "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
People who see their work as a calling report higher life and work satisfaction than those who see it as a job (money) or career (advancement). Worth asking honestly which orientation each offer maps onto for you.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003) — "Affective Forecasting"**
People systematically overestimate how strongly and how long future events — including job changes — will affect their happiness. This cuts both ways: you'll likely adapt to the higher salary faster than you think, but you may also overestimate how miserable "less interesting" work will feel, especially if other aspects (leading a team) are fulfilling.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011) — "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review)**
Analyzing ~12,000 diary entries, the single biggest driver of positive inner work life was making progress in *meaningful* work. Interestingness and meaning aren't identical — a "less interesting" role where you visibly make progress and grow people can score high here.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001) — "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work"**
Employees actively reshape task and relational boundaries of their roles, often making initially unappealing jobs more engaging. Relevant caveat: how interesting a job is at offer time is not fixed, and leads often have more crafting latitude.

**Bottom line per the literature:** your friends are right that interest is underweighted relative to salary, but the strongest predictors of long-run satisfaction are a *bundle* — interest fit, autonomy, growth, meaning, and relationships. The real question the research points to is whether your unfulfilled desire to lead is itself an intrinsic motivator you've been deferring.

prompt:
I'm working as a Research Engineer in OpenAI doing very interesting stuff. I got a proposal from Meta - a bit higher salary, leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do, but much less interesting. I wonder what to do. 

My two good friends claim that research shows doing interesting things is actually underrated and one should focus on this almost exclusively. Is that true? Please respond with only relevant paper names and two-sentence summaries.

reasoning: I'm thinking through which real papers I can cite on this topic—I want to avoid making up fake research. There's solid work from Kahneman on affective forecasting and miswanting, the self-determination theory research from Deci and Ryan on intrinsic motivation, Hackman and Oldham's job characteristics model, and Kahneman and Deaton's findings on money and happiness. I should also consider Amabile's work on the progress principle, Wrzesniewski's research on callings, and the hedonic adaptation literature. I'm continuing through the research papers—adding work on calling orientation and job satisfaction, affective forecasting on how we mispredict happiness, meta-analyses on intrinsic motivation and pay's weak link to satisfaction, the honeymoon-hangover effect after job changes, and considering whether to include research on promotion effects and managerial aspirations.
answer:
**Evidence supporting your friends' claim:**

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) — "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature"**
A meta-analysis of 86 studies found the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak (r ≈ .15). People at very different salary levels report similar satisfaction, suggesting compensation differences are systematically overweighted in job decisions.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976) — "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"**
The foundational job characteristics model shows that skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback drive internal motivation and satisfaction. Intrinsic features of the work itself are among the strongest predictors of sustained engagement.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014) — "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"**
Across 183 studies, intrinsic motivation strongly predicts performance quality, while extrinsic incentives matter more for quantity-type tasks. For creative, research-style work, intrinsic interest is the dominant performance driver.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005) — "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want"**
People systematically mispredict how future circumstances will affect their happiness, adapting quickly to changes like salary increases. Day-to-day experiential factors (what you actually do) tend to matter more than people forecast.

**Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy (2005) — "The Relationship Between Employee Job Change and Job Satisfaction: The Honeymoon-Hangover Effect"**
Job switchers experience a satisfaction spike that erodes within about a year as the new role's realities set in. The initial appeal of a new offer often overstates its long-term value.

**Evidence complicating the claim:**

**Deci & Ryan (2000) — "The 'What' and 'Why' of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior"**
Well-being depends on three needs: autonomy, competence, *and* relatedness—not interest alone. A long-held desire like leading a team can fulfill genuine competence and growth needs, which the "only interestingness matters" view ignores.

**Benson, Li & Shue (2019) — "Promotions and the Peter Principle"**
Using data on ~40,000 workers, the best individual contributors often make worse managers, and firms over-promote based on current-role performance. Worth reading before assuming team leadership will be as satisfying as imagined—but also a reminder that management is a distinct skill worth testing.

**Killingsworth (2021) — "Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 Per Year"**
Contrary to the famous Kahneman & Deaton plateau finding, experienced well-being continues rising with income with no clear cutoff. Salary isn't irrelevant, though the effect is logarithmic—small raises buy little happiness.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997) — "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
People who see work as a calling report higher life and work satisfaction than those with job (money) or career (advancement) orientations. Notably, the *career* orientation—chasing promotions and titles—doesn't predict greater satisfaction.

**Bottom line from the literature:** your friends are directionally right—interestingness and intrinsic motivation are better long-term predictors of satisfaction and performance than pay—but "almost exclusively" overstates it, since autonomy, growth, and tested (not imagined) fit for management also matter.

answer:
**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) — "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)**
Across 92 samples, pay level correlated only weakly (~.15) with job satisfaction, suggesting raises buy far less satisfaction than people expect. This supports the idea that salary differences between otherwise good offers shouldn't dominate the decision.

Higher income improves how people evaluate their lives but stops improving day-to-day emotional experience beyond a moderate threshold. For someone already well-paid (e.g., a research engineer at a top lab), a marginal salary bump likely affects daily happiness little.

**Deci & Ryan (2000) — "The 'what' and 'why' of goal pursuits: Self-determination theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation" (Psychological Inquiry)**
Sustained motivation and well-being depend on satisfying needs for autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not just task interestingness. Notably, leading a team can serve these needs too, which complicates a pure "interestingness only" rule.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014) — "Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives jointly predict performance: A 40-year meta-analysis" (Psychological Bulletin)**
Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts performance quality, but extrinsic incentives matter too, and the two operate jointly rather than one being "the answer." This directly undercuts the "almost exclusively" framing your friends used.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976) — "Motivation through the design of work: Test of a theory" (Organizational Behavior and Human Performance)**
Job satisfaction is driven by multiple job characteristics: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback — interestingness is only part of the picture. A leadership role can score high on several of these dimensions even if the subject matter is duller.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997) — "Jobs, careers, and callings: People's relations to their work" (Journal of Research in Personality)**
People who view their work as a calling report higher life and work satisfaction than those with job or career orientations. This is the strongest evidence on your friends' side — meaningful, engaging work is a major satisfaction driver.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001) — "Crafting a job: Revisioning employees as active crafters of their work" (Academy of Management Review)**
People actively reshape task boundaries and meaning to make work more engaging over time. Relevant because "less interesting" work isn't fixed — a leadership role gives you latitude to craft it.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003) — "Affective forecasting" (Advances in Experimental Social Psychology)**
People systematically overestimate the intensity and duration of emotional reactions to future events (the impact bias). Both your prediction that boring work will make you miserable and that the salary/title will make you happy are likely exaggerated.

**Gilovich & Medvec (1995) — "The experience of regret: What, when, and why" (Psychological Review)**
In the long run, people regret inactions more than actions — particularly unfulfilled ambitions. Relevant because team leadership is "something you always wanted," and forgoing it may sting more over time than a suboptimal job year.

Bottom line per the literature: interestingness/intrinsic motivation is genuinely a heavyweight factor (your friends are directionally right), but "almost exclusively" overstates the evidence — autonomy, growth toward valued goals, and long-held aspirations carry comparable weight, while the salary difference likely matters least.

Job satisfaction is driven by skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback — not just intrinsic interest in the subject matter. Relevant to you: leading a team can boost autonomy and significance, which are independent satisfaction drivers.

Analyzing ~12,000 work diary entries, the single biggest driver of positive inner work life was making progress on meaningful work. Less interesting work where progress stalls could be a real well-being cost.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005) — "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want"**
People systematically overestimate how long and intensely future events (like raises or promotions) will affect their happiness. Worth applying skepticism to *both* predictions — that the salary/title will thrill you, and that "less interesting" work will bother you as much as you fear.

The foundational Job Characteristics Model shows that skill variety, task significance, and autonomy are core drivers of intrinsic motivation and satisfaction. Interesting, meaningful task content is treated as a first-order determinant of work outcomes, not a nice-to-have.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014) — "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"** (Psychological Bulletin)

People systematically overestimate how long and how intensely future events (like a raise or a title) will affect their happiness, and adapt to them faster than expected. **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature"** (*Journal of Vocational Behavior*)
Across 92 studies, pay level correlated with job satisfaction at only about r = .15, meaning salary explains very little of how happy people are at work. This supports your friends' view that chasing a modest pay bump is unlikely to improve your day-to-day satisfaction.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-determination theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation, social development, and well-being"** (*American Psychologist*)
Foundational work showing that autonomy, competence, and relatedness drive sustained motivation and well-being, while extrinsic rewards alone do not. Notably, leading a team you've always wanted to lead could itself serve autonomy and competence needs—intrinsic motivation isn't only about how "interesting" the technical content is.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the design of work: Test of a theory"** (*Organizational Behavior and Human Performance*)
The Job Characteristics Model identifies skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback as the core drivers of internal motivation and satisfaction. People who view their work as a calling report higher life and job satisfaction than those who view it as a job (money) or career (advancement). Worth asking honestly which orientation the Meta offer appeals to—if it's mainly the career framing, this research suggests caution.

answer:
**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"** — Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts well-being and performance, but it's driven by three needs: autonomy, competence, *and* relatedness — not interest alone. Notably, leading a team can satisfy competence and relatedness needs, so "interesting" isn't the only intrinsic factor in your decision.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"** — Across 92 samples, pay level correlated only ~.15 with job satisfaction. A modestly higher salary is unlikely to move your day-to-day satisfaction much.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"** — Beyond a comfortable income threshold, more money improves how you evaluate your life on paper but not your daily emotional experience. **Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (and *The Progress Principle*)** — Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found the single biggest driver of motivation and positive inner work life is making progress on meaningful work. Interesting work matters largely *because* it makes progress feel meaningful — a less interesting role where you progress visibly as a leader could still score well here.

But orientation is partly about how you frame the role, not just the role's content — team leadership can become a calling too.

**Hsee, Zhang, Yu & Xi (2003), "Lay Rationalism and Inconsistency Between Predicted Experience and Decision"** — When choosing, people systematically overweight hard, quantifiable attributes (salary, title) and underweight experiential ones (how the work actually feels), then regret it. This is the strongest evidence in your friends' favor: the Meta offer's advantages are the quantifiable kind people are biased toward.

Bottom line per the literature: your friends are directionally right (interest/meaning beats marginal pay), but "almost exclusively" overstates it — autonomy, growth, relationships, and whether leadership is a genuine aspiration of yours all carry real weight.

The honest takeaway from the literature is that while intrinsic interest matters significantly, it's not the whole story—other factors like compensation, work environment, and relationships also play important roles. I'm adding more papers to round out the list — Judge et al. on pay's weak correlation with satisfaction, Kahneman & Deaton on income and well-being, O'Keefe et al. on developing passion rather than just finding it, Grant on relational job design, Hackman & Oldham's classic job characteristics model, and Wilson & Gilbert on affective forecasting. The verdict crystallizes: intrinsic interest matters significantly but not exclusively — autonomy, relatedness, growth, and person-environment fit all play substantial roles, while compensation has surprisingly minimal impact on satisfaction.
answer:
**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) — "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature"** (Journal of Vocational Behavior)
A meta-analysis of 115 studies found the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak (r ≈ .15). This supports the part of your friends' claim that salary differences matter less than people assume.

**Deci & Ryan (2000) — "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"** (American Psychologist)
Sustained motivation and well-being depend on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. This cuts against "almost exclusively": a team-lead role offering autonomy and growth could satisfy needs that interesting-but-constrained work doesn't.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976) — "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"** (Organizational Behavior and Human Performance)
The Job Characteristics Model shows job satisfaction comes from a bundle: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. "Interestingness" is just one input, and leading a team can increase several of the others.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011) — "The Power of Small Wins"** (Harvard Business Review, based on the diary study in *The Progress Principle*)
Analyzing ~12,000 daily diary entries, they found the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress on meaningful work. Notably, the key variable is *progress and meaning*, not raw intellectual interest — a stalled-but-fascinating project can feel worse than a less glamorous one where you're advancing.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997) — "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"** (Journal of Research in Personality)
People who see work as a calling report higher life and job satisfaction than those with job or career orientations. Partial support for your friends — but "calling" is about meaning and how you frame your work, which is somewhat portable across roles.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018) — "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"** (Psychological Science)
People who believe passions are fixed give up faster when work gets hard, while those who believe interest develops sustain engagement across domains. This suggests the Meta role might become more interesting than it looks now — interest is partly cultivated, not just discovered.

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005) — "Consequences of Individuals' Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis"** (Personnel Psychology)
Person–job and person–organization fit strongly predict satisfaction, commitment, and retention. The relevant question isn't "which job is more interesting in the abstract" but which one fits your values and what you want to become (e.g., a manager vs. an IC researcher).

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003) — "Affective Forecasting"** (Advances in Experimental Social Psychology)
People systematically mispredict how much future circumstances will affect their happiness, overweighting salient features of a choice. Both "this work is so interesting" and "I've always wanted to lead a team" are exactly the kind of salient features we tend to over- and under-weight.

---

**Bottom line:** Your friends are half right. Research strongly supports prioritizing intrinsically motivating work over pay — but not "almost exclusively." Autonomy, progress, growth (like the leadership you've always wanted), and fit are comparably powerful predictors, and interest itself often develops in roles that initially seem less exciting.

I'm adding more papers to round out the argument — Nickerson et al. on financial aspirations and their link to lower satisfaction, O'Keefe et al. on how passion is developed rather than discovered, Kristof-Brown et al. on person-job fit, and Ryan & Deci's Self-Determination Theory covering autonomy, competence, and relatedness. I'm also considering whether to include a closing note that the friends' "almost exclusively" framing overstates the research, which actually shows intrinsic interest matters significantly but isn't the only factor — other elements like fit, autonomy, and growth opportunities play real roles too. I'll add one more citation about how top performers don't always make good managers, then organize everything into thematic groups covering what research supports about finding fulfilling work versus the complications with conventional wisdom.
answer:
**Supporting your friends' claim (interest/intrinsic motivation matters a lot):**

- **Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999), "A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation"** (Psychological Bulletin). A meta-analysis of 128 studies showing that extrinsic rewards like pay can actually undermine intrinsic motivation for tasks. Suggests chasing salary at the expense of interest carries a real psychological cost.

- **Amabile (1985), "Motivation and Creativity: Effects of Motivational Orientation on Creative Writers"** (JPSP). Found that people primed with extrinsic motives produced measurably less creative work than those focused on intrinsic enjoyment. Relevant if your performance as a researcher depends on creativity.

- **Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"** (Journal of Research in Personality). People who experience their work as a "calling" report higher life and job satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. The effect held even within the same occupation.

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"** (PNAS). Day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus at relatively modest income levels, though life evaluation keeps rising. At OpenAI/Meta salary levels, you're far past where marginal pay buys much daily happiness. (Note: Killingsworth 2021 found a smaller plateau effect, so this is contested.)

- **Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want"** (Current Directions in Psychological Science). People systematically overestimate how much external changes (raises, promotions) will affect their happiness, due to "impact bias" and rapid adaptation. The salary bump will likely feel normal within months; daily work content adapts less.

**Complicating the "almost exclusively" part:**

- **O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"** (Psychological Science). Interest is not fixed — people who believe passions are developed rather than found sustain engagement better when work gets hard. The Meta role might become interesting; the OpenAI role might not stay interesting.

- **Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"** (American Psychologist). Well-being depends on three needs — autonomy, *competence*, and *relatedness* — not just task interest. Leading a team (a long-held goal of yours) could satisfy growth and relatedness needs that pure IC research work doesn't.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005), "Consequences of Individuals' Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis"** (Personnel Psychology). Job satisfaction is best predicted by overall person-job and person-organization *fit*, a multidimensional construct including values, needs, and abilities. Interest is one input among several, not a single dominant factor.

- **Benson, Li & Shue (2019), "Promotions and the Peter Principle"** (QJE). Strong individual contributors are often promoted into management where they perform worse, and firms knowingly trade this off. A useful caution: wanting to lead a team and being fulfilled by leading one are different things — worth testing your assumption.

*Honest summary: the research supports "interest is underrated relative to pay," but not "focus on it almost exclusively" — autonomy, fit, and your genuine desire to lead matter too, and the last paper suggests verifying that desire before betting on it.*

answer:
**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (American Psychologist)**
Foundational work showing that intrinsic motivation (interest, enjoyment) is a powerful driver of performance, persistence, and well-being. However, it also identifies autonomy, competence, and *relatedness/growth* as core needs — leading a team could satisfy needs that interesting solo work doesn't.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis" (Psychological Bulletin)**
Meta-analysis of 183 studies finding intrinsic motivation is a medium-to-strong predictor of performance, especially for quality of work (like research). Crucially, it finds intrinsic and extrinsic motivators *jointly* predict outcomes — supporting "interest matters a lot," not "interest matters exclusively."

**Wrzesniewski et al. (2014), "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets" (PNAS)**
Longitudinal study of 10,000+ cadets showing internal motives predicted long-term success, while strong instrumental motives (status, money) actually *undermined* outcomes even when internal motives were also present. One of the strongest pieces of evidence for your friends' position.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)**
Found pay level correlates with job satisfaction at only r ≈ .15 — people earning very different salaries report similar satisfaction. Suggests "a bit higher salary" should carry little weight in your decision.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" (Journal of Research in Personality)**
People who view work as a "calling" report higher life and work satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. Notably, the "career" orientation (advancement, leadership) sits in between — relevant since team leadership is something you've "always wanted."

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" (Psychological Science)**
Challenges the "follow your existing interest" framing: people with a growth mindset about interests develop passion for new domains and persist better through difficulty. A counterpoint — the Meta role could *become* interesting, especially the leadership component you're drawn to.

**Iyengar, Wells & Schwartz (2006), "Doing Better but Feeling Worse: Looking for the 'Best' Job Undermines Satisfaction" (Psychological Science)**
Job-seeking "maximizers" landed jobs with ~20% higher salaries but were less satisfied with them. Relevant warning about optimizing on comparable external metrics like salary.

**Verdict:** Your friends are directionally right — the evidence strongly supports weighting interest/intrinsic motivation over modest salary differences — but "almost exclusively" overstates it, since the literature also shows leadership and growth opportunities satisfy core motivational needs, and passions can be developed in new roles.

Ryan & Deci's foundational work on self-determination theory shows that intrinsic motivation depends on autonomy, competence, and relatedness—not just interest. Wrzesniewski's research on callings demonstrates that viewing work as meaningful predicts satisfaction, while Amabile & Kramer's work highlights how progress in meaningful tasks fuels motivation. The income research is mixed: Kahneman & Deaton found diminishing returns for emotional well-being above a threshold, though Killingsworth's more recent analysis suggests income continues to matter even at higher levels. O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton challenge the "follow your passion" narrative by showing interests can be developed rather than discovered. Person-environment fit research, particularly Kristof-Brown's meta-analysis, indicates alignment between individual values and job characteristics matters significantly. Judge's meta-analysis on pay and satisfaction found only weak correlations, suggesting money alone doesn't drive satisfaction but remains a factor.
answer:
**Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999) — "A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation"**
This meta-analysis of 128 studies found that tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for interesting tasks. It supports the idea that intrinsic interest is a powerful and somewhat fragile driver of engagement.

**Ryan & Deci (2000) — "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"**
The foundational SDT paper argues that well-being depends on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and *relatedness*—not interest alone. Notably, leading a team could satisfy autonomy and relatedness needs that pure individual-contributor research work might not.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) — "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"**
Across 86 studies, pay level correlated only weakly with job satisfaction (r ≈ .15). This is probably the strongest empirical support for your friends' position that a modest salary bump shouldn't weigh heavily.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997) — "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
People who view their work as a calling (intrinsically meaningful) report significantly higher life and work satisfaction than those who view it as a job (money) or career (advancement). Interestingly, orientation was somewhat independent of the actual occupation.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018) — "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"**
This paper challenges the "fixed passion" model: people who believe interests are developed rather than discovered show more resilience and can cultivate interest in new domains. This cuts *against* your friends' claim—the Meta role could become interesting, especially the leadership component you've always wanted.

**Vallerand et al. (2003) — "Les Passions de l'Âme: On Obsessive and Harmonious Passion"**
Distinguishes harmonious passion (work integrated healthily into life) from obsessive passion (work that crowds out everything else), with only the former predicting well-being. Optimizing "almost exclusively" for interestingness is closer to the obsessive pattern.

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005) — "Consequences of Individuals' Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis"**
Job satisfaction is best predicted by overall person–job and person–organization fit, which includes values, growth opportunities, and demands—not just task interest. Multidimensional fit beat any single factor.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011) — "The Power of Small Wins" (HBR, based on *The Progress Principle*)**
Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress on meaningful work. Note the operative word is *meaningful*, not *interesting*—which could apply to building a team as much as to research.

---

One honest caveat: the literature strongly supports that intrinsic interest matters more than pay, but no body of research supports weighting it "almost exclusively"—autonomy, growth (like the leadership you want), and fit are robust independent predictors. Your friends are directionally right but overstating it.

reasoning: I need to find legitimate research papers on whether interesting work is genuinely underrated and should be prioritized, so I'll focus on well-established studies about intrinsic motivation, job satisfaction, and meaningful work—drawing from foundational researchers like Deci and Ryan's self-determination theory, Amabile's work on the progress principle, and Wrzesniewski's research on callings and job crafting. I'm realizing the framing "almost exclusively" needs nuance—research shows intrinsic motivation matters significantly, but income and wellbeing do correlate more than that phrasing suggests, especially recent work showing no plateau effect. I'll stick to the requested format of paper names with two-sentence summaries, including studies from both sides so the evidence itself conveys the complexity, maybe with a brief closing note to acknowledge the nuance. I'll keep the bottom line concise.
answer:
**Wrzesniewski, Schwartz, Cong, Kane, Omar & Kolditz (2014), "Multiple types of motives don't multiply the motivation of West Point cadets" (PNAS)**
Tracked ~11,000 cadets and found internal motives (genuine interest in the work) predicted career success, while instrumental motives (pay, status) not only failed to help but actively weakened the benefit of internal motives. Often cited as the strongest evidence for prioritizing intrinsic interest.

**Cerasoli, Nickerson & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives jointly predict performance: A 40-year meta-analysis" (Psychological Bulletin)**
Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance, especially for quality of work, but extrinsic incentives matter too, particularly for quantity-based outcomes. Supports "interest matters a lot" but not "exclusively."

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)**
Across ~90 samples, pay level correlated with job satisfaction at only r ≈ .15 — surprisingly weak. Suggests a modest salary bump is unlikely to meaningfully change how you feel about your job.

**Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999), "A meta-analytic review of experiments examining the effects of extrinsic rewards on intrinsic motivation" (Psychological Bulletin)**
Found that tangible rewards can crowd out intrinsic motivation for tasks people already find interesting. Relevant caution about trading interesting work primarily for money.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the design of work: Test of a theory" (Organizational Behavior and Human Performance)**
Job satisfaction is driven by multiple characteristics: skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback — not interestingness alone. Notably, a team-lead role can score higher on autonomy and significance even if the topic is less fascinating.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit theories of interest: Finding your passion or developing it?" (Psychological Science)**
People who believe interests are fixed give up on new domains quickly, while those who believe interests can be developed find engagement in unfamiliar areas. Implies "less interesting" work can become interesting — and treating current interest as fixed is itself a bias.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective forecasting: Knowing what to want" (Current Directions in Psychological Science)**
People systematically mispredict how happy future choices will make them, overweighting salient features (like a fascinating project) and underweighting daily realities (colleagues, autonomy, growth). Both your forecast of "interesting" and "boring" are probably exaggerated.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000 per year" (PNAS)**
Contrary to the famous plateau finding, day-to-day well-being keeps rising log-linearly with income. A counterpoint showing money is not as irrelevant as the "passion-only" framing suggests.

Bottom line: the literature strongly supports weighting intrinsic interest heavily — but "almost exclusively" overstates it, and your friends are ignoring that autonomy, growth (e.g., leading a team), and developability of interest are independently powerful predictors.

answer:
**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) – "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature"**

This review of behavioral economics evidence shows workers treat meaning and mission as a substantial part of compensation, often accepting lower pay for work they find meaningful. It frames meaning as a real economic good, not a soft afterthought.

The classic job characteristics model finds motivation is driven by skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback — not interestingness alone. Notably, the autonomy and responsibility you'd get from leading a team are themselves core motivational ingredients, which cuts against the "almost exclusively" part of your friends' claim.

**Ryan & Deci (2000) – "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"**
Sustained motivation and well-being depend on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Interesting work feeds competence, but a leadership role you've long wanted could feed autonomy and relatedness — the theory doesn't privilege one need over the others.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997) – "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
People who view work as a calling report higher life and job satisfaction than those with job or career orientations, independent of income and occupation. However, the same line of research shows orientation depends partly on how you craft the role, not just the role's intrinsic content.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010) – "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"**
Day-to-day emotional well-being plateaued around ~$75K, while life evaluation kept rising with income. At your likely income level, the salary bump from Meta would mostly buy life-evaluation points, not daily happiness — though see the next paper.

**Killingsworth (2021) – "Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year"**

**Schkade & Kahneman (1998) – "Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction"**
Whatever attribute you're focusing on while deciding (interestingness, salary, title) gets dramatically overweighted relative to its actual impact on later experience. Worth noting this applies symmetrically: your friends may be inducing a focusing illusion on "interestingness" itself.

People systematically overestimate both the intensity and duration of emotional reactions to future events, including job changes. Both the thrill of a new leadership role and the pain of less interesting work will likely fade faster than you predict.

**Benson, Li & Shue (2019) – "Promotions and the Peter Principle"**

**Short answer embedded in the evidence:** your friends are directionally right (intrinsic interest and meaning beat marginal salary), but "almost exclusively" overstates it — autonomy, growth, relationships, and the leadership opportunity itself are independently strong predictors of satisfaction.

People who view their work as a calling rather than a job or career report significantly higher work and life satisfaction, even within the same occupation. This suggests intrinsic engagement with the work itself matters more than status or pay markers.

**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being** (Deci & Ryan, 2000, *American Psychologist*)
Argues that well-being and sustained motivation depend on satisfying needs for autonomy, competence, and relatedness — interest matters, but so do growth opportunities like leadership. **The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis** (Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010, *Journal of Vocational Behavior*)
Finds pay level correlates only weakly (~r = .15) with job satisfaction across 92 samples. Supports your friends' view that a modest salary bump shouldn't be the deciding factor.

**Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis** (Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014, *Psychological Bulletin*)
Shows intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, but extrinsic incentives matter too and the two operate jointly rather than in opposition. Directly contradicts the "almost exclusively" part of your friends' claim.

**Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work** (Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997, *Journal of Research in Personality*)

answer:
**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997) — "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
People who view their work as a calling (intrinsically meaningful) report substantially higher job and life satisfaction than those who view it as a job (money) or career (advancement). Orientation toward the work itself predicted satisfaction better than income or occupational prestige.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) — "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"**
Across 86 studies, pay level correlated only weakly (r ≈ .15) with job satisfaction. People at high salary levels were barely more satisfied than those earning far less, suggesting marginal salary differences buy little wellbeing.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010) — "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"**
Day-to-day emotional wellbeing plateaus around ~$75k/year, though life evaluation keeps rising with income. Note: Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023, adversarial collaboration) revised this — for most (non-unhappy) people, wellbeing keeps rising with income, so money isn't irrelevant.

**Brickman, Coates & Janoff-Bulman (1978) — "Lottery Winners and Accident Victims: Is Happiness Relative?"**
Classic demonstration of hedonic adaptation: large financial windfalls produce surprisingly small lasting happiness gains. A raise is the kind of thing people adapt to quickly; daily work content is harder to adapt away from.

**Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999) — "A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation"**
Extrinsic rewards can crowd out intrinsic motivation for interesting tasks. Supports the idea that optimizing for pay over interest can backfire on long-run engagement.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (2014, PNAS) — "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets"**
Cadets with strong internal motives performed better long-term; adding instrumental motives (status, money) on top actually *worsened* outcomes. Evidence that mixed motives can dilute the benefits of intrinsic interest.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014) — "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"**
Counterpoint to your friends: intrinsic motivation predicts performance quality, but extrinsic incentives independently matter, especially for quantity-based outcomes. Both contribute; "almost exclusively" overstates the evidence.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018) — "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"**
Interests are substantially *developed*, not just discovered — people with a "fixed" theory of passion give up faster when work gets hard. Relevant because the Meta role (leading a team, a long-held goal of yours) could become interesting as you grow into it.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003/2005) — "Affective Forecasting" / "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want"**
People systematically mispredict how future circumstances will make them feel, overweighting salient features (salary, title) at decision time. A reason to be cautious about both your forecasts: "interesting forever" and "managing will fulfill me."

**Bottom line in two sentences:** The literature supports your friends' direction — intrinsic interest predicts satisfaction far better than marginal pay differences — but not the "almost exclusively" part, since incentives, autonomy, growth, and fit all show independent effects. Also note the leadership opportunity is itself an intrinsic motive (a long-held aspiration), so this isn't a clean interesting-vs-money tradeoff.

answer:
**Papers supporting your friends' claim:**

**Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999) — "A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation"**
This meta-analysis of 128 experiments found that tangible rewards tend to undermine intrinsic motivation for interesting tasks. It's a cornerstone of the argument that chasing pay over interest can backfire motivationally.

**Wrzesniewski, Schwartz et al. (2014) — "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets" (PNAS)**
Tracking over 10,000 cadets, internal motives (genuine interest) predicted long-term success, while holding strong instrumental motives (money, status) alongside them actually weakened outcomes. Suggests "interesting + a bit more pay" doesn't simply stack.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) — "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"**
Across 86 studies, the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction was only about r = .15. A "bit higher salary" is unlikely to move your satisfaction much.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010) — "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being" (PNAS)**
Day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus well below typical OpenAI/Meta compensation levels. At your income range, marginal salary differences are nearly irrelevant to felt happiness.

**Papers complicating the "almost exclusively" part:**

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014) — "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"**
Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts performance quality, but extrinsic factors still independently predict performance — they're complements, not substitutes. Argues against treating interest as the *only* variable.

**Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007) — "Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features"**
This meta-analysis of work design shows satisfaction is driven by a bundle of factors: autonomy, task variety, significance, feedback, and social characteristics — not interest alone. Leading a team (autonomy, social impact) hits several of these dimensions directly.

**Ryan & Deci (2000) — "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"**
Well-being depends on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — "interestingness" is only one route to satisfying them. A leadership role you've always wanted may serve autonomy and relatedness better than a more interesting individual-contributor role.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018) — "Implications of Fixed and Growth Theories of Interest"**
People who believe interests are fixed give up on new domains quickly; those with a "growth theory of interest" develop passion in initially less-interesting areas. The Meta role may become interesting once you're invested — and leadership itself is something you say you've always wanted.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005) — "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want"**
People systematically mispredict how happy future situations will make them, overweighting salient features (like "interestingness") and underestimating adaptation. Worth applying to both options before deciding.

**Bottom line per the literature:** your friends are roughly half right — interest beats marginal salary by a wide margin, but "almost exclusively" overstates it; autonomy, growth, and the leadership opportunity you've long wanted are heavyweight factors in the same research.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"** (*Psychological Bulletin*)

Interests are substantially developed through engagement rather than pre-fixed, and people with a "fixed theory of interest" give up faster when work gets hard. A counterpoint to your friends: the Meta role could become interesting, especially via the new challenge of leadership.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"** (*PNAS*)
Day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around ~$75K/year, while life evaluation keeps rising with income. At your likely compensation level, the marginal salary bump from Meta probably affects daily happiness very little.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 Per Year"** (*PNAS*)
A counterpoint using real-time sampling of 33K+ adults: well-being continues rising log-linearly with income with no plateau. So the salary difference isn't fully dismissible, though the effect of *log* income means a "bit higher" salary buys very little.

People systematically mispredict how future circumstances will make them feel, overestimating both the intensity and duration of emotional reactions. Caution for both directions: you may overestimate how much "less interesting work" will hurt and how much "finally leading a team" will help.

Firms promote their best individual contributors into management even though IC performance poorly predicts managerial performance. Relevant caution on the leadership appeal: wanting to manage and thriving as a manager are different things — worth testing cheaply before betting a job change on it.

Short answer: your friends are overstating it — the literature says intrinsic interest matters a lot but interacts with autonomy, growth, and meaning, and interest itself is more malleable than people assume.

**Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M. (2000). "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being." *American Psychologist.***

answer:
**Wrzesniewski, A., Schwartz, B., et al. (2014). "Multiple types of motives don't multiply the motivation of West Point cadets." PNAS.**
Tracking 10,000+ cadets over 14 years, intrinsic motives (genuine interest in the work) predicted better career outcomes, while instrumental motives (money, status) predicted worse outcomes—even when held alongside intrinsic ones. Supports your friends' view: instrumental reasons may actively dilute the benefits of interest.

**Cerasoli, C., Nicklin, J., & Ford, M. (2014). "Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives jointly predict performance: A 40-year meta-analysis." Psychological Bulletin.**
Across 183 studies, intrinsic motivation strongly predicted performance quality, but extrinsic incentives independently predicted quantity-based performance—both mattered. Counters the "almost exclusively" framing: motivation is not zero-sum.

**Judge, T., et al. (2010). "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis." Journal of Vocational Behavior.**
Pay level correlated with job satisfaction at only r ≈ .15 across 86 studies—people earning very different salaries reported similar satisfaction. Suggests the salary bump alone is a weak reason to move.

**Amabile, T. (1985). "Motivation and creativity: Effects of motivational orientation on creative writers." JPSP.**
Writers primed to think about extrinsic reasons for writing produced measurably less creative work than those primed with intrinsic reasons. Relevant if creative research output is what you care about maximizing.

**O'Keefe, P., Dweck, C., & Walton, G. (2018). "Implicit theories of interest: Finding your passion or developing it?" Psychological Science.**
People who believe interests are fixed give up faster when work gets difficult; those who believe interests are developed sustain engagement in new domains. Complicates your friends' advice: the Meta role might *become* interesting, especially since team leadership is a long-held goal.

**Wrzesniewski, A., & Dutton, J. (2001). "Crafting a job: Revisioning employees as active crafters of their work." Academy of Management Review.**
Employees substantially reshape the meaning and engagement of their jobs through how they craft tasks and relationships—interestingness is partly endogenous, not fixed in the job description. A leadership role may give you more crafting latitude, not less.

**Wilson, T., & Gilbert, D. (2005). "Affective forecasting: Knowing what to want." Current Directions in Psychological Science.**
People systematically mispredict the intensity and duration of future emotional states, including how much they'll enjoy or regret career choices. Your prediction that the Meta work is "much less interesting"—and that this will dominate your experience—is itself uncertain.

**Bottom line:** the research strongly supports weighting intrinsic interest heavily, but not "almost exclusively"—and your stated long-term desire to lead a team is itself an intrinsic motive, not just an instrumental one.

Foundational paper arguing that well-being and sustained motivation depend on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. Notably, leading a team can satisfy autonomy and relatedness needs, so your Meta offer isn't purely an "extrinsic" option.

Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, but extrinsic incentives matter too and the two coexist rather than one dominating. This supports "interest matters a lot" but undercuts "focus on it almost exclusively."

Pay level correlates with job satisfaction at only about r = .15, meaning salary differences explain very little of how satisfied people feel at work. This is the strongest evidence for your friends' position — a "bit higher salary" is likely the least decision-relevant factor on your list.

The user asked for just paper names and two-sentence summaries, so I should stick to that format and let the research speak for itself rather than adding editorial commentary. The key insight is that while intrinsic interest matters significantly, framing it as "almost exclusively" misses the mark — autonomy and growth opportunities, like those gained through team leadership, are equally powerful drivers.
answer:
**Woolley & Fishbach (2015), "The Experience Matters More Than You Think: People Value Intrinsic Incentives More Inside Than Outside an Activity" (JPSP).** People systematically underweight how much intrinsic enjoyment will matter when choosing future activities, but once actually doing the work, enjoyment dominates persistence and satisfaction. This is the strongest direct support for your friends' claim that "interestingness" is underrated at decision time.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis" (Journal of Vocational Behavior).** Across 86 studies, pay level correlated only about r = .15 with job satisfaction and r = .23 with pay satisfaction itself. A modestly higher salary is a weak predictor of being happier in a role.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "The 'What' and 'Why' of Goal Pursuits: Self-Determination Theory" (Psychological Inquiry).** Well-being at work depends on satisfying needs for autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. Notably, leading a team can serve these needs (especially autonomy and growth), so the Meta offer isn't purely an "extrinsic" option.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis" (Psychological Bulletin).** Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts the quality of work performance, but extrinsic incentives matter too, particularly for quantity-based outcomes. The evidence supports "interest matters a lot," not "focus on it almost exclusively."

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory" (Organizational Behavior and Human Performance).** Job satisfaction is driven by five characteristics: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. Interestingness maps mainly onto one or two of these; a leadership role can score higher on autonomy and significance even if the content is duller.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review).** Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making daily progress on meaningful work. This favors whichever job offers clearer progress and meaning to you — which may or may not be the "more interesting" one.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work" (Academy of Management Review).** People substantially reshape how interesting and meaningful their jobs are after taking them. This cuts against treating "interestingness as advertised" as a fixed property of either offer.

**Gilbert & Wilson (2000), "Miswanting: Some Problems in the Forecasting of Future Affective States" (in *Feeling and Thinking*).** People reliably mispredict how future choices will make them feel, overweighting salient features (like a title or a prestigious project) and underweighting daily texture. A caution applicable to both options — and to your friends' confidence in a single decision rule.
prompt:
I'm working as a Research Engineer in OpenAI doing very interesting stuff. I got a proposal from Meta - a bit higher salary, leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do, but much less interesting. I wonder what to do. 

My two good friends claim that research shows doing interesting things is actually underrated and one should focus on this almost exclusively. Is that true? Please respond with only relevant paper names and two-sentence summaries.
